seoTitle: How to Add Emojis in Microsoft Loop (2026)
metaDescription: Insert inline emojis and custom page cover icons in Microsoft Loop using the colon shortcut or the header icon picker for more scannable pages.
intro: A page full of plain text can feel cold and hard to scan. This guide shows how to add expressive emojis inside your Loop text and how to give a page a themed cover icon, both of which make dense documentation easier to navigate visually.

steps.heading: Place your cursor where you want an emoji
steps.body: Open your target Loop page, place your cursor on any line, and enter edit mode.

steps.heading: Use the colon shortcut
steps.body: Type a colon followed immediately by a keyword, like :fire, to filter matching emoji symbols.

steps.heading: Insert the emoji
steps.body: Press Enter or click the filtered graphic from the dropdown selector to insert it inline.

steps.heading: Change a page's header icon
steps.body: Hover over the main page title and click the Add Icon button to open the header asset picker.

steps.heading: Pick a theme icon
steps.body: Select your decorative graphic to update both the page header and the left sidebar directory entry at the same time.

faq.question: Do emojis look the same across different devices in Loop?
faq.answer: Loop uses universal emoji font layers so symbols stay visually consistent across platforms and devices.

faq.question: What if the colon shortcut doesn't bring up the emoji picker?
faq.answer: If a browser extension conflict blocks the shortcut, you can fall back to your native Windows or macOS emoji keyboard panel.

faq.question: Can I use an emoji as a page's icon instead of just inline text?
faq.answer: Yes, clicking the page header's Add Icon button lets you set a decorative emoji as the page's cover icon in the sidebar.

faq.question: Is it okay to use a lot of emojis in professional documentation?
faq.answer: It's best to balance visual highlights with clean text so the page stays scannable rather than cluttered.
